CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (new SOTA on PE dataset, comparison of parsing vs tagging, and benefits of MTL). Writing quality is reported as good and the methodology and evaluation setup are clearly described with thorough related work coverage. However the summary omits details such as whether notation and hyperparameters are fully specified and whether figures/tables are present and maximally informative; the absence of reported hyperparameters slightly reduces clarity for reproduction. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear intro→methods→experiments→conclusion flow and the main contributions (SOTA on PE, comparative framings, and benefits of MTL) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and the writing quality is described as good; related work coverage is thorough which aids readability. Figures, tables and examples are reported as effective and informative, though the paper has minor presentation gaps (e.g., no mention of released code or hyperparameter listings) that slightly hinder full reproducibility rather than clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (new SOTA on PE dataset, comparison of parsing vs tagging, and benefits of MTL). Writing quality is reported as good and the methodology and evaluation setup are clearly described with thorough related work coverage. However the summary omits details such as whether notation and hyperparameters are fully specified and whether figures/tables are present and maximally informative; the absence of reported hyperparameters slightly reduces clarity for reproduction.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear intro→methods→experiments→conclusion flow and the main contributions (SOTA on PE, comparative framings, and benefits of MTL) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and the writing quality is described as good; related work coverage is thorough which aids readability. Figures, tables and examples are reported as effective and informative, though the paper has minor presentation gaps (e.g., no mention of released code or hyperparameter listings) that slightly hinder full reproducibility rather than clarity.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper does not propose a fundamentally new model or theoretical framework but rather applies and adapts existing neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER) and framing choices (token-based dependency parsing vs. sequence tagging) to argumentation mining. Its main novelty is empirical: showing that simpler local BiLSTM tagging models and multi-task learning outperform parsing-based formulations on the Persuasive Essays dataset; however this is evaluated on a single dataset without new principled analysis or theoretical justification. The authors clearly differentiate their empirical comparisons from prior ILP and parser baselines, but the techniques amount to non-trivial engineering and empirical evaluation rather than a substantially new method, so the contribution is moderately incremental. | B(3): From an empirical-pragmatist view the paper offers moderate novelty: it systematically applies and compares existing neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF variants, adapted LSTM-ER) and MTL to end-to-end argumentation mining and shows the useful finding that token-based dependency parsing is inferior for this task. The work differentiates its contributions clearly from prior feature-based and parsing baselines and provides a practical perspective that simpler local tagging models are robust — a useful but incremental methodological message. However, the techniques are largely reapplications and adaptations of known NN methods rather than fundamentally new algorithms or formalisms, so the contribution is informative to practitioners but not highly original.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper does not propose a fundamentally new model or theoretical framework but rather applies and adapts existing neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER) and framing choices (token-based dependency parsing vs. sequence tagging) to argumentation mining. Its main novelty is empirical: showing that simpler local BiLSTM tagging models and multi-task learning outperform parsing-based formulations on the Persuasive Essays dataset; however this is evaluated on a single dataset without new principled analysis or theoretical justification. The authors clearly differentiate their empirical comparisons from prior ILP and parser baselines, but the techniques amount to non-trivial engineering and empirical evaluation rather than a substantially new method, so the contribution is moderately incremental.

reviewer_b.justification: From an empirical-pragmatist view the paper offers moderate novelty: it systematically applies and compares existing neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF variants, adapted LSTM-ER) and MTL to end-to-end argumentation mining and shows the useful finding that token-based dependency parsing is inferior for this task. The work differentiates its contributions clearly from prior feature-based and parsing baselines and provides a practical perspective that simpler local tagging models are robust — a useful but incremental methodological message. However, the techniques are largely reapplications and adaptations of known NN methods rather than fundamentally new algorithms or formalisms, so the contribution is informative to practitioners but not highly original.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a useful, practical contribution by reporting state-of-the-art results on the Persuasive Essays dataset and showing that simpler BiLSTM-CRF and MTL approaches are strong, actionable alternatives to more complex formulations. However, they also concur that the empirical validation is limited to a single benchmark and lacks deeper analysis or released details, which constrains claims of broad generality. Given the clear practical value and likelihood of influencing modeling choices despite these limits, I assign a score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem (end-to-end argumentation mining) and reports state-of-the-art neural results on the Persuasive Essays dataset, demonstrating that simpler BiLSTM-CRF variants and MTL are competitive versus dependency-parsing framings. However, the empirical contribution is narrowly anchored to a single dataset (PE) without theoretical analysis, bounds, or discussion of failure modes, which weakens claims about generality. The finding that token-based dependency parsing is subpar and that local tagging models work well is useful for practitioners, but the lack of broader empirical validation or principled justification limits the work to moderate impact rather than a field-changing contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles the important practical problem of end-to-end argumentation mining and reports new state-of-the-art results on the standard Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset, which will matter to practitioners. Its empirical finding—that simpler BiLSTM-CRF tagging models (and MTL of natural subtasks) outperform token-based dependency parsing formulations and adapted entity-relation models—is a clear, actionable result that should change modeling choices in the area. While the work is narrowly evaluated on one benchmark and lacks released hyperparameters/code in the summary, the strong empirical comparisons to well-known baselines and parsers make the contributions likely to be adopted and built upon by researchers and engineers.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides comprehensive and fair comparisons against an appropriate set of baselines using the same dataset and metrics, and that the analyses of different model framings are informative. I rate it 4 because, while the empirical comparisons are strong and well-motivated, the evaluation is limited to a single dataset and the paper lacks reported hyperparameters/code and statistical testing, which weakens reproducibility and the strength of comparative claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper benchmarks extensively against a wide set of relevant baselines (feature-based ILP from Stab & Gurevych 2016, Persing & Ng evaluation, multiple dependency parsers, the BLCC sequence tagger, and adapted LSTM-ER) and uses the same PE dataset and Persing & Ng metrics, so comparisons are largely fair. The authors acknowledge when certain baselines (dependency-parsing framing) perform worse and analyze differences between tagging, parsing and MTL setups. I deduct one point because evaluation is limited to a single dataset, hyperparameters/code are not reported (reducing reproducibility), and there is no indication of statistical testing or broader generalization analysis that would strengthen the comparative claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a comprehensive set of appropriate baselines (feature-based ILP from Stab & Gurevych 2016, Persing & Ng setup, multiple dependency parsers, STagBLCC sequence tagger, and an adapted LSTM-ER), all evaluated on the same Persuasive Essays dataset with the same Persing & Ng metrics. The authors run multiple model families (parsers, BiLSTM-CRF variants, MTL setups) and analyze why certain framings (token-based dependency parsing) perform worse while simpler BiLSTM taggers and joint MTL improve results, and they discuss prior work thoroughly—meeting my empirical emphasis on broad, fair, and practical comparisons.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is well within the NLP/ML venue scope, uses appropriate methods and baselines, and produces strong empirical results on the standard Persuasive Essays dataset. The main weaknesses are limited empirical scope (single dataset) and missing reproducibility details (code/hyperparameters), which justify a slight downgrade but do not outweigh the paper’s clear relevance and contribution, so I mark it as a 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—end-to-end neural approaches for computational argumentation mining—is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and targets the right audience by comparing modern neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER) and parsers against established baselines on the Persuasive Essays dataset. However, the work is primarily empirical and evaluated on a single dataset (PE, 402 essays with 80 test), with no reported hyperparameter details or code and limited theoretical analysis of why simpler tagging models capture long-range argument structure; from my theoretical-minimalist perspective this weakens generalization claims. Evaluation metrics and baselines are appropriate and well positioned in the community, but the lack of multi-dataset validation, principled justification, and reproducibility details make the fit borderline for a top-tier venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper studies a clearly NLP-centred problem (end-to-end argumentation mining) and reports new state-of-the-art results on the standard Persuasive Essays dataset while comparing against a wide set of strong baselines (feature-based ILP, multiple dependency parsers, BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER). The framing (token-based tagging and parsing, plus MTL experiments) and the evaluation protocol follow community standards, so the audience and methods match a top NLP/ML venue. My only reservation is practical reproducibility: code and hyperparameters are not reported, which weakens the paper’s utility for practitioners and the empirical-rigor standards I prize.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical claims are supported by extensive experiments and strong baselines on the PE dataset, and that the main findings (tagging models outperforming dependency-style formulations and benefits from joint training) are credible. Remaining weaknesses are methodological: evaluation on a single dataset, missing hyperparameter/code release, and lack of statistical significance testing, which limit robustness and generalizability but do not invalidate the core empirical contributions. Given the strength of the experiments balanced with these reproducibility concerns, a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main empirical claims (BiLSTM tagging models outperform dependency-parsing framing and MTL helps) are supported by experiments on the PE dataset and by comparisons to reasonable baselines (ILP, multiple parsers, LSTM-ER). However, the evaluation is limited to a single dataset (Persuasive Essays), hyperparameters and statistical significance testing are not reported, and no theoretical or principled analysis is offered to justify why the simpler models generalize to long-range AM phenomena. These methodological omissions raise concerns about the robustness and generalizability of the claims, so I cannot rate it higher.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main empirical claims are clearly stated and supported by extensive experiments comparing a wide range of strong baselines (feature ILP, multiple dependency parsers, BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER) on the PE dataset, and the results back the assertions about tagging models outperforming token-based dependency parsing and benefits from joint (MTL) training. There are no formal mathematical proofs to check. My main technical concerns are reproducibility and rigor of experimental reporting: hyperparameters and code release are not mentioned and no statistical significance testing is reported, which weakens confidence in some comparisons. These are minor relative to the empirical scope and do not invalidate the primary conclusions, so I rate it a 4.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents a solid empirical comparison across multiple neural architectures and discusses negative results, but is limited by evaluation on a single dataset, sparse ablation/diagnostic analysis, and missing hyperparameter details or released code which hurt reproducibility and generalization claims. Given these strengths and the concrete empirical contributions, but weighing the reproducibility and analysis shortcomings, a mid-level score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental comparison is reasonably thorough in model variety (parsers, taggers, LSTM-ER, BiLSTM-CRF variants) and uses the established PE dataset and Persing & Ng evaluation, but it is limited to a single dataset and lacks reported hyperparameters or code, which weakens reproducibility and generalization claims. Ablation/sensitivity analyses appear minimal beyond comparing different model families, and no statistical significance testing or multi-dataset validation is reported — shortcomings I penalize given the paper's empirical focus. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and some negative results/limitations (e.g., poor performance of token-based dependency parsing, leaving encoder–decoder as future work) are discussed, but failure-mode analyses and diagnostic explanations for why models work are insufficient. Overall the evidence supports the immediate empirical claims on PE but falls short of the stronger generalization and theoretical justification I require.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs a broad set of experiments comparing multiple neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF variants, LSTM-ER) against strong baselines (feature-based ILP, various dependency parsers) on the PE dataset, and evaluates MTL vs single-task setups — giving solid empirical support for the main claims. Ablations are present in the form of model variants and MTL comparisons, but the work omits some analyses (e.g., encoder–decoder framing) and does not report hyperparameters or release code, which weakens reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and the authors explicitly discuss negative results (poor performance of token-based dependency parsing). Overall the empirical evaluation is strong but falls short on reproducibility details.

main_claims: The paper achieves new state-of-the-art results in end-to-end argumentation mining on the Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset from Stab and Gurevych (2016).
Framing argumentation mining as token-based dependency parsing yields subpar performance compared to other approaches.

Less complex local tagging models based on BiLSTMs (e.g., BiLSTM-CRF and variants) perform robustly across classification scenarios and can capture long-range dependencies inherent to argumentation mining.
Jointly learning natural subtasks in a multi-task learning (MTL) setup improves performance over single-task models.
_summary.methodology: The authors investigate neural end-to-end approaches by framing argumentation mining as both token-based dependency parsing and token-based sequence tagging, including multi-task learning; they evaluate BiLSTM-based tagging models (including BiLSTM-CRF and BiLSTM-CRF-CNN variants) and adapt a joint entity-relation model (LSTM-ER) for the task.
datasets: Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset from Stab and Gurevych (2016) (402 essays, 80 reserved for testing)
baselines: Feature-based ILP model from Stab and Gurevych (2016)
Dependency parsers: MST, Mate, Dyer et al. (LSTM-Parser), Kiperwasser and Goldberg (Kiperwasser)
Sequence tagger: BLCC tagger from Ma and Hovy (2016) (referred to as STagBLCC)
LSTM-ER (Miwa and Bansal, 2016) adapted for argumentative entities and relations
metrics: Component and relation F1 scores computed from TP, FP, FN as in Persing and Ng (2016)
Level α matching for components (e.g., exact 100% span match or 50% approximate match) as described in Persing and Ng (2016)
limitations: Token-based dependency parsing framing produces subpar performance for the AM task.
The encoder-decoder framing for AM is not considered/evaluated in this work and is left as future work.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - Computational Argumentation Mining (end-to-end neural approaches)

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Minimalist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a PhD in machine learning theory; experience publishing formal analyses of model properties and guarantees.
traits: Penalises empirical papers that lack formal or theoretical justification for model choices (e.g., bounds, proofs, or principled analyses).
Deducts points if the paper's main novelty is empirical tuning or showing that simpler methods work on one dataset without generalization arguments.
Requires strict statistical testing and multiple datasets; subtracts score if evaluation is limited to a single dataset or missing significance tests.
Penalises papers that do not analyze failure modes or provide interpretable/diagnostic analyses explaining why the approach works.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on conceptual contribution and principled justification; gives high scores only when theoretical insights or strong generalization arguments accompany empirical results.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Pragmatist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in NLP with extensive experience building end-to-end neural systems and publishing experimental papers demonstrating state-of-the-art results across tasks.
traits: Rewards clear empirical improvements and strong baselines: adds credit when a paper reports new state-of-the-art on a relevant benchmark with well-implemented baselines.
Values thorough experimental methodology and ablation studies: increases score when multiple model variants, MTL setups, and ablations are evaluated and compared.
Prefers work that emphasizes engineering, reproducibility, and practical applicability (e.g., release of code, hyperparameters, runtime/efficiency comparisons).
Gives positive bias to papers that simplify pipelines (showing simpler models that perform robustly) and that advocate for practical modeling choices over complex formulations.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Emphasizes empirical rigor, reproducibility, and practical impact; rewards papers that advance what practitioners can reliably use even if theoretical analysis is limited.
